title: Pissaladière
subtitle: Niçoise onion tart with anchovy and black olive.
excerpt: A flat bread base spread with slow-cooked onions, criss-crossed with anchovy fillets, and dotted with black olives. Provence in a tray bake. Cuts into squares for an apéritif or wedges for lunch.
sourceNotes: Drawn from the documented Niçois tradition recorded in the Cuisine niçoise canon, with onion-confit timing and assembly following the slow-cooked method preserved through Provençal market-bakery practice.

content.text: Two kilos of onions cooked down for an hour with thyme until they collapse into a sweet, dark spread. A simple olive-oil dough rolled out thin onto a baking tray. The onions spread over the dough; anchovy fillets criss-crossed in a lattice; one Niçoise olive in each diamond gap. Twenty minutes in a hot oven and you have lunch.

The dish takes its name from pissalat, the fermented anchovy paste once spread directly onto the dough. The modern version uses whole tinned anchovies, which are easier to find and give a stronger visual.
A large rectangular tin around 30 by 25 cm, oiled. The onions need a wide heavy saucepan to spread out properly.

Set the saucepan over medium heat. Pour in {{olive-oil}} of olive oil, then tip in {{onion}} of sliced onions, {{thyme}} of thyme, {{bay-leaves}} of bay, and a generous pinch of salt. Stir to coat. Cook uncovered for 60 minutes, stirring every 10 minutes or so. The onions release their water, collapse, then darken to a deep amber. Stir in {{garlic}} of chopped garlic for the last 5 minutes. Pluck out the thyme stalks and bay. Let cool while the dough proves.

In a large bowl, mix {{strong-bread-flour}} of flour, {{yeast-dried}} of yeast, and {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt. Pour in {{olive-oil}} of olive oil and {{water}} of warm water. Stir with a wooden spoon until shaggy, then knead in the bowl for 5 minutes, or until smooth and elastic. Cover with a tea towel and leave in a warm spot for 60 minutes until doubled.

Heat the oven to 220°C. Oil the baking tray. Knock back the dough and stretch it out into the corners of the tray, pressing with your fingertips so the surface dimples. If it springs back, leave it for 5 minutes and try again. Spread the cooled onions across the dough in an even thick layer. Lay the anchovy fillets in a diamond lattice over the onions. Drop {{olives-black}} of olives into the gaps.
Bake for 20 to 25 minutes until the dough underneath the onions is fully cooked and the edges of the dough are deep gold. Pull out and let stand 10 minutes before cutting into squares.

items.fix: Use a wide saucepan so the moisture evaporates. Keep the heat at medium for the full hour and stir to keep the bottom from sticking.
items.cause: Heat too low or the pan too crowded.
items.symptom: Onions turned mushy, not deeply caramelised

items.fix: Drain the cooled onions in a sieve for 10 minutes before spreading. The juices can be reduced separately and spooned over later if you don't want to lose them.
items.cause: Onions too wet when spread on the dough.

items.fix: Use 24 fillets for a 30 by 25 cm tray, criss-crossed at the lattice spacings; don't double up.
items.cause: Too many for the size of tray.

items.fix: Niçoise olives are sold with stones in. Tell guests, or use pitted Kalamatas as a substitute.
items.cause: Used pitted olives, where the warning context is lost.

content.text: Pissaladière is bakery food in Nice and the small towns along the Côte d'Azur. The proper version sits in a tray on the bakery counter, cut into squares with a long knife, sold by weight. At home it works as an apéritif with a glass of chilled rosé, or as the centrepiece of a picnic alongside cold roast chicken and a salad of green leaves dressed with anchovy.

recipe.yieldDescription: One 30 x 25 cm tray, cut into 12 squares.
